          Programming note: Money Stuff will be off tomorrow through Tuesda=
y, back next Wednesday, May 31.  As you know, Money Stuff=E2=80=99s time of=
f is correlated with lower market volatility. I take this to be bullish for=
 debt-ceiling negotiations, though of course this is not investing advice. =
Anyway, good luck with all that, see you Wednesday.

 =20
   =20
      Debt ceiling stuff
   =20
 =20

I kind of don=E2=80=99t get this:

The tension around the US debt-limit negotiations ratcheted up after Fitch =
Ratings warned the nation=E2=80=99s AAA rating was under threat from a poli=
tical standoff that=E2=80=99s preventing a deal.
Fitch may downgrade its assessment to reflect the increased partisanship th=
at is hindering a resolution despite the fast-approaching so-called X date,=
 it said, referring to the point at which Washington runs out of cash. It m=
oved the US to =E2=80=9Crating watch negative=E2=80=9D under its classifica=
tion. Meantime, DBRS Morningstar placed the US ratings of AAA under review =
=E2=80=9Cwith negative implications.=E2=80=9D =E2=80=A6
The cost of insuring US sovereign debt against default with derivatives has=
 risen, and Fitch=E2=80=99s statement also casts the spotlight on other rat=
ing agencies to see how they might react. A White House spokesperson said t=
he report demonstrated the urgency of reaching a speedy resolution. =E2=80=
=A6
Moody=E2=80=99s Investors Service, which has the US at a AAA rating with a =
stable outlook, hasn=E2=80=99t made any adjustments in wake of the wranglin=
g in Washington to lift the nation=E2=80=99s borrowing capacity. The firm=
=E2=80=99s base case is also that a deal will be made before the x-date.
S&P has also retained a stable outlook on the rating during the latest frac=
as, long anticipating that Congress would ultimately raise the debt ceiling=
 before the Treasury lost its ability to fully fund the government. The fir=
m continues to stand by that view as last presented in a March 16 report, a=
 spokesperson said on Thursday.
The Fitch warning is =E2=80=9Ccertainly very symbolic, and in a way it may =
force Moody=E2=80=99s to follow suit,=E2=80=9D said Vishnu Varathan, head o=
f economics and strategy at Mizuho Bank Ltd. in Singapore. =E2=80=9CIt will=
 also place more scrutiny on the dollar and Treasuries as havens and its ri=
sk-free rate qualities.=E2=80=9D

That is from   Ruth Carson's Bloomberg story on potential ratings cuts due =
to the US debt ceiling negotiations. There are=C2=A0two possibilities here:
=09A Fitch (or Moody=E2=80=99s, S&P, etc.) downgrade will have regulatory o=
r quasi-regulatory consequences. Insurance companies and banks will no long=
er be allowed to hold AA+ rated US Treasury bonds, or will have to have mor=
e capital against them, so there will be mass selling of Treasuries and hug=
e holes in banks=E2=80=99 and insurers=E2=80=99 balance sheets. Bond mutual=
 funds and money-market funds that are required by their mandates to hold o=
nly very safe bonds will no longer be able to hold AA+ rated Treasuries, le=
ading to more mass selling. Various collateral regimes that only accept AAA=
 rated collateral will no longer accept Treasuries, leading to more sales a=
nd a shortage of safe collateral. Much of the financial system=C2=A0runs on=
=C2=A0a supply of trillions of dollars of AAA rated Treasuries, which serve=
 as safe-haven assets and as risk-free collateral, and if they are downgrad=
ed chaos will ensue. Or,=09That won=E2=80=99t happen, and everyone who owns=
 Treasuries today will still be allowed to hold them after a Fitch (or whoe=
ver) downgrade. Oh they might not=C2=A0want=C2=A0to =E2=80=94=C2=A0they mig=
ht say =E2=80=9Chmm Fitch makes some good points here that the debt ceiling=
 is coming up, better sell Treasuries=E2=80=9D =E2=80=94=C2=A0but they woul=
d not be=C2=A0forced=C2=A0to sell. Fitch would be just one source of advice=
 and analysis for investors to consider, not an arbiter whose judgments hav=
e the force of law.

Which one do you think is true? Which one do you think=C2=A0ought=C2=A0to b=
e true?
My sense is that Option 1 is mostly wrong: Few investors have any sort of m=
andate that is like =E2=80=9Cyou can only buy AAA-rated bonds, and if Treas=
uries are downgraded to AA+ you have to dump them.=E2=80=9D
One way to tell this is by reading some rules. The  rules for money market =
funds, for instance, used to require money market funds to buy only highly =
rated assets, but they were  revised in 2015 to remove references to credit=
 ratings. Now funds can buy an asset=C2=A0as long as they make=C2=A0a =E2=
=80=9Cdetermination that it presents minimal credit risks at the time the f=
und acquires the security.=E2=80=9D They also have to =E2=80=9Cprovide ongo=
ing review of whether each security (other than a government security) cont=
inues to present minimal credit risks=E2=80=9D: They have to keep evaluatin=
g the issuers of commercial paper to see if they have become riskier, but=
=C2=A0they don=E2=80=99t have to do that for Treasuries. Treasuries are in =
their own separate category, above petty worries about creditworthiness.
Similarly, the  collateral rules for the Federal Reserve=E2=80=99s discount=
 window require investment-grade ratings (BBB- or better) for US dollar cor=
porate bonds, and AAA ratings for foreign-currency corporate bonds. But US =
Treasuries are just US Treasuries; they are always eligible and there are n=
o ratings criteria. The  Bank of England also accepts US government bonds c=
ategorically as Level A collateral for its liquidity insurance schemes. The=
 US Commodity Futures Trading Commission=E2=80=99s  rules for derivatives m=
argin also say that US government securities are eligible collateral with n=
o ratings criteria.=C2=A0
Or for  bank capital, the rule is that a bank =E2=80=9Cmust assign a zero p=
ercent risk weight to an exposure to the U.S. government, its central bank,=
 or a U.S. government agency.=E2=80=9D For insurance capital, the National =
Association of Insurance Commissioners  sets standards for risk-based capit=
al based in part on ratings;  but there is=C2=A0=E2=80=9Cno [risk-based cap=
ital]=C2=A0requirement for bonds guaranteed by the full faith and credit of=
 the United States =E2=80=A6 because it is assumed that there is no default=
 risk associated with U.S. Government issued securities.=E2=80=9D Nothing a=
bout ratings.
You can keep doing this, though I won=E2=80=99t; I assume that at some poin=
t you will come across=C2=A0something=C2=A0that says that someone can only =
hold AAA rated securities, and so a ratings-agency downgrade will force the=
m to sell. But it does not seem to be the norm.
The better way to tell this is just by   looking at what=C2=A0happened last=
 time:
In 2011, S&P Global Ratings drew fire for downgrading the US from AAA after=
 a similar brush with default. That spurred a selloff in risk assets like e=
quities around the world, but ironically boosted Treasuries as investors so=
ught out havens.
If an S&P downgrade forced a lot of investors to sell Treasuries in 2011, t=

   =20
 =20

The theory is pretty straightforward:
=09Banks are  a confidence business. If people trust that a bank is safe an=
d sound, they will keep their money there; if they don=E2=80=99t, they won=
=E2=80=99t.=09If people all pull their money out of a bank, it won=E2=80=99=
t be safe and sound: It will have to sell off long-term assets to pay back =
depositors, it will get a bad price, and it won=E2=80=99t have enough to pa=
y them all.
  [2]

=09So confidence in banks is self-fulfilling, as is a lack of confidence.=
=09The most high-profile public indication of confidence in a publicly trad=
ed company is its stock price.=09If the stock price goes down, people will =
think that a company is in trouble.=09For most companies, this doesn=E2=80=
=99t matter that much: If it is not in trouble, it can ignore its stock pri=
ce and just do its business and eventually the stock will sort itself out.=
=09But for banks, it does matter: If it looks like a bank is in trouble, pe=
ople will pull their money out, and it will be in trouble.=09If it gets in =
enough trouble, its stock will go to zero.=09Retail investors posting on me=
ssage boards can gang up with their friends and move stock prices.=09If the=
y bet against a bank=E2=80=99s stock, and then gang up with their friends t=
o move that bank=E2=80=99s stock down, then they can destroy the bank and m=
ake a lot of money.

Is this theory right? Well. I mean, much of it is kind of right, but you co=
uld object to bits of it. Does a falling stock price undermine confidence i=
n a bank? Sometimes, probably, but the point of bank deposits is that they =
are  supposed to be information insensitive, and it is possible that a lot =
of depositors in sleepy US regional banks are not constantly checking Robin=
hood to see how their bank=E2=80=99s stock is doing. Can retail investors o=
n message boards gang up to move stock prices? That seems to be=C2=A0the le=
sson of the  2020 meme-stock mania, but that might have been a very specifi=
c moment =E2=80=94 Covid lockdowns, bored traders, novelty =E2=80=94 that c=
an=E2=80=99t be repeated arbitrarily.
Ultimately though this is not a very interesting theoretical question; it i=
s an empirical question. If Reddit retail day traders bring down a bank, th=
en we=E2=80=99ll know that Reddit retail day traders can bring down a bank.=
 If they don=E2=80=99t, then we won=E2=80=99t.
  [3]

Have they? I mean, I think the answer is no. Some US regional banks have fa=
iled recently, but not, as far as I now, after being particularly targeted =
by retail traders on social media. Credit Suisse Group AG was kind of targe=
ted by retail traders on social media last year =E2=80=94 we talked about i=
t; I called it a =E2=80=9Creverse meme stock=E2=80=9D =E2=80=94 and then  i=
t did fail this March due to deposit flight, but it does not seem like the =
deposit flight was caused by=C2=A0the social-media posting.
But the experiment is ongoing. Gunjan Banerji  reports at the Wall Street J=
ournal:

A near-record number of shares in regional banks has changed hands this mon=
th, and the number of options bets against the sector has exploded. Big Wal=
l Street institutions and at-home individual investors alike are taking par=
t, feeding on each other=E2=80=99s selling and buying.
Rookie traders are flooding social media with doomsday theories on the next=
 bank that might fail. Others are loading up on bank stocks, speculating th=
at there will soon be an epic jump in shares.
The result? A market that moves from calm to chaotic faster than ever. ...
Before March, individual investors didn=E2=80=99t pay much attention to reg=
ional banks. Compared with GameStop, AMC or Tesla, they were way too boring=
.
Stock gyrations can be punishing for any company, but they can be fatal for=
 banks, where confidence is currency. Although a plunging share price shoul=
dn=E2=80=99t have any immediate effect on a bank=E2=80=99s ability to satis=
fy its financial obligations, it can drive depositors to withdraw their mon=
ey, which does.
=E2=80=9CYou can create real panic,=E2=80=9D said Cem Karsan, founder of Ch=
icago-based investment firm Kai Volatility Advisors.

The article features =E2=80=9CDaniel Betancourt, a 30-year-old pub owner in=
 Pomona, Calif.,=E2=80=9D who buys puts on bank stocks and talks about it o=
n Discord, and who scored a big win on PacWest Bancorp earlier this month:

One way to think about stock buybacks is that, if you are a $10 billion com=
pany, and you think that in=C2=A0the future you will be a $50 billion compa=
ny, you should buy back stock. Your business has a lot of great opportuniti=
es to make money, so your stock is a good investment. It is cheap today and=
 will be expensive later; if you buy stock then you will make money on the =
trade.
Another way to think about stock buybacks is that, if you are a $10 billion=
 company, and you think that in the future you will be a $5 billion company=
, you should buy back stock. If your business is shrinking and there are=C2=
=A0not=C2=A0a lot of great opportunities to make money, then your company=
=C2=A0doesn=E2=80=99t need to be as big as it once was. The business doesn=
=E2=80=99t need the amount of capital that it once did; you do not need to =
reinvest in capital equipment and research and development if your market i=
s shrinking. There is money today, you don=E2=80=99t have any great uses fo=
r it, you might as well return it to shareholders. And in the US in 2023, t=
he normal way to return money to shareholders is by buying back stock.
If another dollar invested in your business =E2=80=94=C2=A0in R&D or capita=
l expenditures or whatever =E2=80=94=C2=A0would have a 50%=C2=A0return, the=
n you should invest some dollars in your business, but you might also be te=
mpted to buy back some stock because you are bullish that the stock will go=
 up. If another dollar invested in your business would have a 0% return, th=
en you shouldn=E2=80=99t really invest a lot of dollars in your business, a=
nd if you have a bunch of dollars you might as well spend them on buybacks.
And so if a lot of companies are buying back a lot of stock, but nobody=C2=
=A0else=C2=A0is buying a lot of stock, that=C2=A0could=C2=A0mean that corpo=
rate managers=C2=A0think that their future looks bright and that they will =
have great performance over the next few years, while professional investor=
s disagree. Or it=C2=A0could=C2=A0mean that=C2=A0everybody=C2=A0thinks that=
 the future looks kinda meh and companies will not have great returns over =
the next few years; professional investors act on that view by selling stoc=
k, while corporate managers act on that view by returning capital to shareh=
olders because they don=E2=80=99t have any better use for it.=C2=A0
The  Wall Street Journal reports:

Obviously many of the specific stories here are along the lines of =E2=80=
=9Cour business is great, we are rolling in money, we just cannot possibly =

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;"><strong>Programming note:</strong> <em>Money S=
tuff will be off tomorrow through Tuesday, back next Wednesday, May 31. <a =
href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/31597233.273415/aHR0c=
HM6Ly9wYXBlcnMuc3Nybi5jb20vc29sMy9wYXBlcnMuY2ZtP2Fic3RyYWN0X2lkPTQzODYyNTY/=
607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB94445bee" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"item=
scope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !im=
portant; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bot=
tom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> As you know</a>, Money Stuff=E2=
=80=99s time off is correlated with lower market volatility. I take this to=
 be bullish for debt-ceiling negotiations, though of course this is not inv=
esting advice. Anyway, good luck with all that, see you Wednesday.</em></p>
<table class=3D"header" width=3D"100%" border=3D"0" cellspacing=3D"0" cellp=
adding=3D"0">
<tr>
<td class=3D"body-header-container" style=3D"border-collapse: collapse; mso=
-table-lspace: 0pt; mso-table-rspace: 0pt;">
<h2 style=3D"font-weight: bold; font-family: "Arial Black", Arial=
, Helvetica, sans-serif; letter-spacing: -1px; font-size: 20px; border-bott=
om-width: 1px; border-bottom-style: dotted; margin: 0px; padding: 16px 0px =
5px;">Debt ceiling stuff</h2>
</td>
</tr>
</table>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I kind of don=E2=80=99t get this:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The tension around the US debt-limit negotiati=
ons ratcheted up after Fitch Ratings warned the nation=E2=80=99s AAA rating=
 was under threat from a political standoff that=E2=80=99s preventing a dea=
l.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Fitch may downgrade its assessment to reflect =
the increased partisanship that is hindering a resolution despite the fast-=
approaching so-called X date, it said, referring to the point at which Wash=
ington runs out of cash. It moved the US to =E2=80=9Crating watch negative=
=E2=80=9D under its classification. Meantime, DBRS Morningstar placed the U=
S ratings of AAA under review =E2=80=9Cwith negative implications.=E2=80=9D=
 =E2=80=A6</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The cost of insuring US sovereign debt against=
 default with derivatives has risen, and Fitch=E2=80=99s statement also cas=
ts the spotlight on other rating agencies to see how they might react. A Wh=
ite House spokesperson said the report demonstrated the urgency of reaching=
 a speedy resolution. =E2=80=A6</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Moody=E2=80=99s Investors Service, which has t=
he US at a AAA rating with a stable outlook, hasn=E2=80=99t made any adjust=
ments in wake of the wrangling in Washington to lift the nation=E2=80=99s b=
orrowing capacity. The firm=E2=80=99s base case is also that a deal will be=
 made before the x-date.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">S&P has also retained a stable outlook on =
the rating during the latest fracas, long anticipating that Congress would =
ultimately raise the debt ceiling before the Treasury lost its ability to f=
ully fund the government. The firm continues to stand by that view as last =
presented in a March 16 report, a spokesperson said on Thursday.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The Fitch warning is =E2=80=9Ccertainly very s=
ymbolic, and in a way it may force Moody=E2=80=99s to follow suit,=E2=80=9D=
 said Vishnu Varathan, head of economics and strategy at Mizuho Bank Ltd. i=
n Singapore. =E2=80=9CIt will also place more scrutiny on the dollar and Tr=
easuries as havens and its risk-free rate qualities.=E2=80=9D</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">That is from <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloo=
mbergbusiness.com/click/31597233.273415/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9=
uZXdzL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjMtMDUtMjQvdW5pdGVkLXN0YXRlcy1tYXktYmUtY3V0LWJ5LWZpdG=
NoLXJhdGluZ3Mtb24tZGVidC1saW1pdC1maWdodD9jbXBpZD1CQkQwNTI1MjNfTU9ORVlTVFVGR=
iZ1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsJnV0bV9zb3VyY2U9bmV3c2xldHRlciZ1dG1fdGVybT0yMzA1MjUm=
dXRtX2NhbXBhaWduPW1vbmV5c3R1ZmY/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB87e69548" itemprop=
=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #0=
00000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-b=
ottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">=
  Ruth Carson's Bloomberg story</a> on potential ratings cuts due to the US=
 debt ceiling negotiations. There are=C2=A0two possibilities here:</p>
<ol style=3D"list-style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;"=
>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">A Fitch (or Moody=E2=80=99s, S&P,=
 etc.) downgrade will have regulatory or quasi-regulatory consequences. Ins=
urance companies and banks will no longer be allowed to hold AA+ rated US T=
reasury bonds, or will have to have more capital against them, so there wil=
l be mass selling of Treasuries and huge holes in banks=E2=80=99 and insure=
rs=E2=80=99 balance sheets. Bond mutual funds and money-market funds that a=
re required by their mandates to hold only very safe bonds will no longer b=
e able to hold AA+ rated Treasuries, leading to more mass selling. Various =
collateral regimes that only accept AAA rated collateral will no longer acc=
ept Treasuries, leading to more sales and a shortage of safe collateral. Mu=
ch of the financial system=C2=A0<em>runs on=C2=A0</em>a supply of trillions=
 of dollars of AAA rated Treasuries, which serve as safe-haven assets and a=
s risk-free collateral, and if they are downgraded chaos will ensue. Or,</l=
i>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">That won=E2=80=99t happen, and every=
one who owns Treasuries today will still be allowed to hold them after a Fi=
tch (or whoever) downgrade. Oh they might not=C2=A0<em>want=C2=A0</em>to =
=E2=80=94=C2=A0they might say =E2=80=9Chmm Fitch makes some good points her=
e that the debt ceiling is coming up, better sell Treasuries=E2=80=9D =E2=
=80=94=C2=A0but they would not be=C2=A0<em>forced=C2=A0</em>to sell. Fitch =
would be just one source of advice and analysis for investors to consider, =
not an arbiter whose judgments have the force of law.</li>
</ol>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Which one do you think is true? Which one do y=
ou think=C2=A0<em>ought=C2=A0</em>to be true?</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">My sense is that Option 1 is mostly wrong: Few=
 investors have any sort of mandate that is like =E2=80=9Cyou can only buy =
AAA-rated bonds, and if Treasuries are downgraded to AA+ you have to dump t=
hem.=E2=80=9D</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">One way to tell this is by reading some rules.=
 The <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/31597233.2734=
15/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuZWNmci5nb3YvY3VycmVudC90aXRsZS0xNy9jaGFwdGVyLUlJL3BhcnQt=
MjcwL3NlY3Rpb24tMjcwLjJhLTc/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBf3a4a71a" itemprop=3D"=
StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #00000=
0; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-botto=
m-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> rul=
es for money market funds</a>, for instance, used to require money market f=
unds to buy only highly rated assets, but they were <a href=3D"https://link=
.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/31597233.273415/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2VjLmdvd=
i9uZXdzL3ByZXNzLXJlbGVhc2UvMjAxNS0xOTM/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBbd840776" i=
temprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"co=
lor: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; b=
order-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: =
#ccc;"> revised in 2015</a> to remove references to credit ratings. Now fun=
ds can buy an asset=C2=A0as long as they make=C2=A0a =E2=80=9Cdetermination=
 that it presents minimal credit risks at the time the fund acquires the se=
curity.=E2=80=9D They also have to =E2=80=9Cprovide ongoing review of wheth=
er each security (other than a government security) continues to present mi=
nimal credit risks=E2=80=9D: They have to keep evaluating the issuers of co=
mmercial paper to see if they have become riskier, but=C2=A0<em>they don=E2=
=80=99t have to do that for Treasuries</em>. Treasuries are in their own se=
parate category, above petty worries about creditworthiness.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Similarly, the <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bl=
oombergbusiness.com/click/31597233.273415/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuZnJiZGlzY291bnR3a=
W5kb3cub3JnL1BhZ2VzL0NvbGxhdGVyYWwvY29sbGF0ZXJhbF9lbGlnaWJpbGl0eQ/607f07cee=
f4b8524a319a23cB6f746c0e" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" ta=
rget=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; =
border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style=
: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> collateral rules</a> for the Federal Res=
erve=E2=80=99s discount window require investment-grade ratings (BBB- or be=
tter) for US dollar corporate bonds, and AAA ratings for foreign-currency c=
orporate bonds. But US Treasuries are just US Treasuries; they are always e=
ligible and there are no ratings criteria. The <a href=3D"https://link.mail=
.bloombergbusiness.com/click/31597233.273415/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmFua29mZW5nbG=
FuZC5jby51ay8tL21lZGlhL2JvZS9maWxlcy9tYXJrZXRzL2VsaWdpYmxlLWNvbGxhdGVyYWwvc=
3VtbWFyeS10YWJsZS1vZi1jb2xsYXRlcmFsLnBkZg/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB95260b8d=
" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D=
"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px=
; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-colo=
r: #ccc;"> Bank of England</a> also accepts US government bonds categorical=
ly as Level A collateral for its liquidity insurance schemes. The US Commod=
ity Futures Trading Commission=E2=80=99s <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloom=
bergbusiness.com/click/31597233.273415/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuZWNmci5nb3YvY3VycmVu=
dC90aXRsZS0xNy9jaGFwdGVyLUkvcGFydC0yMy9zdWJwYXJ0LUUvc2VjdGlvbi0yMy4xNTY/607=
f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBfc6a337d" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemsco=
pe" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !impor=
tant; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom=
-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> rules for derivatives margin</a> a=
lso say that US government securities are eligible collateral with no ratin=
gs criteria.=C2=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Or for <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergb=
usiness.com/click/31597233.273415/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cubGF3LmNvcm5lbGwuZWR1L2Nmc=
i90ZXh0LzEyLzMuMzI/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB3f1320b8" itemprop=3D"StoryLink=
" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-d=
ecoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: =
#000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> bank capital=
</a>, the rule is that a bank =E2=80=9Cmust assign a zero percent risk weig=
ht to an exposure to the U.S. government, its central bank, or a U.S. gover=
nment agency.=E2=80=9D For insurance capital, the National Association of I=
nsurance Commissioners <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/c=
lick/31597233.273415/aHR0cHM6Ly9jb250ZW50Lm5haWMub3JnL3NpdGVzL2RlZmF1bHQvZm=
lsZXMvaW5saW5lLWZpbGVzLzIwMjElMjBSZXZpc2lvbnMlMjB0byUyMHRoZSUyMFJCQyUyMEMxJ=
TIwQm9uZCUyMEZhY3RvcnMlMjA1LTI0LnBkZg/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBf798e195" it=
emprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"col=
or: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; bo=
rder-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #=
ccc;"> sets standards</a> for risk-based capital based in part on ratings; =
<a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/31597233.273415/aH=
R0cHM6Ly9jb250ZW50Lm5haWMub3JnL3NpdGVzL2RlZmF1bHQvZmlsZXMvaW5saW5lLWZpbGVzL=
zIwMjItMTUtSCUyMHdlYnBvc3QucGRm/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB3edce426" itemprop=
=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #0=
00000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-b=
ottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">=
 but there is</a>=C2=A0=E2=80=9Cno [risk-based capital]=C2=A0requirement fo=
r bonds guaranteed by the full faith and credit of the United States =E2=80=
=A6 because it is assumed that there is no default risk associated with U.S=
. Government issued securities.=E2=80=9D Nothing about ratings.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">You can keep doing this, though I won=E2=80=99=
t; I assume that at some point you will come across=C2=A0<em>something=C2=
=A0</em>that says that <em>someone</em> can only hold AAA rated securities,=
 and so a ratings-agency downgrade will force them to sell. But it does not=
 seem to be the norm.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The better way to tell this is just by <a href=
=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/31597233.273415/aHR0cHM6L=
y93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9uZXdzL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjMtMDUtMjQvdW5pdGVkLXN0YXRl=
cy1tYXktYmUtY3V0LWJ5LWZpdGNoLXJhdGluZ3Mtb24tZGVidC1saW1pdC1maWdodD9jbXBpZD1=
CQkQwNTI1MjNfTU9ORVlTVFVGRiZ1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsJnV0bV9zb3VyY2U9bmV3c2xldH=

<blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">In 2011, S&P Global Ratings dr=

 That spurred a selloff in risk assets like equities around the world, but =

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">If an S&P downgrade forced a lot of invest=

=A0downgrade in 2023 is not going to lead to much or any forced selling of =

: solid; background-color: #ccc;">  <em>still=C2=A0</em>has Treasuries at A=

a decade, and=C2=A0going from Aaa/AA+/AAA/AAA (at Moody=E2=80=99s, S&P,=

=A0<em>that=C2=A0</em>much.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">So that leaves Option 2: If Fitch, or whoever,=
 downgrades the US government from AAA to AA+, that will not force anyone t=
o do anything, but some investors will wake up and say =E2=80=9Chuh, Fitch =
is worried about a US government default=E2=80=9D and act somehow on Fitch=
=E2=80=99s worries, by selling or more likely buying (!?!) Treasuries, or b=
y doing some more complicated trade to bet on or hedge against a government=
 default.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">And, you know, fine; I am sure that the people=
 at Fitch who are worried about a US government default are smart and have =
been following the matter more closely than=C2=A0<em>I=C2=A0</em>have, and =
if you want to get your analysis of partisan negotiations in Washington fro=
m Fitch I am not going to stop you. But there are at least 10,000 smart peo=
ple who have been following the debt-ceiling negotiations more closely than=
 I have and who are publishing news articles and opinion columns and sell-s=
ide research and newsletters and tweets about this, and it is not=C2=A0<em>=
obvious=C2=A0</em>to me why you would assign more weight to Fitch than to l=
ots of other people. Fitch is not, I hope, in the negotiating room. Fitch=
=E2=80=99s specialized expertise is in credit analysis, not US politics.<a =
class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-1" style=3D"color: #000000; text=
-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color=

<span>[1]</span>
</a>=C2=A0If you read Fitch=E2=80=99s analysis of the debt ceiling and say =
=E2=80=9Chuh, they made some points that I hadn=E2=80=99t thought of, and t=
hey have persuaded me that default is more likely than I thought,=E2=80=9D =
then go ahead and do your default trades. But if you just read the part of =
the analysis that used to say =E2=80=9CAAA (stable)=E2=80=9D and now says =
=E2=80=9CAAA (rating watch negative),=E2=80=9D and that might even soon say=
 =E2=80=9CAA+,=E2=80=9D what are you getting from that analysis? What do yo=
u learn about US politics when an =E2=80=9CA=E2=80=9D changes to a =E2=80=
=9C+=E2=80=9D?</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I mean, don=E2=80=99t get me wrong, it=E2=80=
=99s a thing. Carson:</p>
<blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Fitch=E2=80=99s warning =E2=80=9Cu=
nderscores the need for swift bipartisan action by Congress to raise or sus=
pend the debt limit and avoid a manufactured crisis for our economy,=E2=80=
=9D said Lily Adams, a spokesperson from Treasury.=C2=A0</p></blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">If you are a ratings agency, your views on the=
 default risk of corporate bonds and structured credit can have a quasi-reg=
ulatory effect; what you say changes how investors act. Your views on the d=
efault risk of US government bonds mostly=C2=A0<em>don=E2=80=99t=C2=A0</em>=
have that effect, but they do have some oracular political effect. When Fit=
ch harrumphs that the US government might default, it <a href=3D"https://li=
nk.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/31597233.273415/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuZnQuY29=
tL2NvbnRlbnQvYmIwNTBhNGUtYjRiNS00MTBiLWJhZWYtM2UwMWFlN2IyN2U4/607f07ceef4b8=

=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; bord=
er-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: so=
lid; background-color: #ccc;"> gets headlines</a>, and a Treasury spokesper=
son says stuff about how it underscores the need for swift bipartisan et ce=
tera. When=C2=A0<em>I=C2=A0</em>harrumph that the US government might defau=
lt, or when everyone you see on television and everyone you talk to harrump=
hs that, it doesn=E2=80=99t have the same effect. But as far as I can tell =
the effect is mostly theater.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">My Bloomberg Opinion colleague <a href=3D"http=
s://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/31597233.273415/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuY=

om-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; ba=
ckground-color: #ccc;">  John Authers writes</a>:</p>
<blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">It=E2=80=99s hard to see how any a=
gency could justify keeping the top rating amid the kind of dysfunction now=
 on display in Washington. In theory, Fitch is only recognizing what is alr=
eady obvious; why not make such an adjustment when it knows it would have n=
o choice but to downgrade if there is any kind of default? Even so, the act=
ion might concentrate minds in both Washington and Wall Street.</p></blockq=
uote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">It seems to be true that Fitch=E2=80=99s harru=
mphing has some mind-concentrating effect; I just don=E2=80=99t get why. Al=
so, while I take the point that =E2=80=9Cthe kind of dysfunction now on dis=
play in Washington=E2=80=9D is certainly more suggestive of AA+ functioning=
 than AAA functioning, I cannot really imagine a=C2=A0<em>good=C2=A0</em>wa=
y for Fitch to flip that switch. If they had downgraded the US a month ago =
would that have been helpful? (S&P did it 12 years ago; is that better?=
) Would it have been=C2=A0<em>informative</em>? What if they were like =E2=
=80=9Cehhh actually this dysfunction is more BBB+=E2=80=9D? How precisely c=
an you grade the dysfunction and map it to ratings categories? How does tha=
t help?</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">If I were a ratings agency my rating on US gov=
ernment debt would not be a semi-arbitrary collection of As. My rating woul=
d be=C2=A0=E2=80=9Cthis is US government debt.=E2=80=9D For good and for il=
l, people mostly know what that means!</p>

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">One model you could have is that US Treasury b=
ills and US regional banks compete for savers=E2=80=99 money. If you want t=
o put your cash somewhere safe for a short term, you put it in the bank or =
you buy Treasury bills. In the last few months, Treasury bills were offerin=
g much higher interest rates=C2=A0<em>and=C2=A0</em>looked much safer than =
a lot of regional bank accounts. This was bad for the banks: People took th=
eir money out of the banks and put it into Treasuries, the banks lost their=
 cheap funding, their business got worse and a few of them failed.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But now there is the debt ceiling, and one con=
sequence of that is that Treasury bills, while they are <a href=3D"https://=

;">  Alice Atkins reported</a>:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Trillions of dollars stowed away in short-term=
 US Treasuries are looking anything but safe as the debt ceiling deadline e=
dges closer. That=E2=80=99s prompted an ETF giant to warn that one of the y=
ear=E2=80=99s hottest haven trades could be about to unwind =E2=80=94 and t=

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Lori Heinel, who oversees the biggest short-te=
rm US Treasury ETF as chief-investment officer at State Street Global Advis=

raising the US government=E2=80=99s $31.4 trillion borrowing limit enters i=
ts final days. =E2=80=A6</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=E2=80=9CIf they really do press this to a poi=
nt where there is a technical default or some other extraordinary intervent=
ion, perversely it could create a huge flight of money back into bank depos=
its,=E2=80=9D Heinel said in an interview. =E2=80=9CBanks could be the most=
 immediate beneficiary as people look for alternatives to Treasury funds.=
=E2=80=9D</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I don=E2=80=99t know, but I do think it would =
be very funny if a lot of people were like =E2=80=9Cugh I can't trust the g=
overnment anymore, guess it=E2=80=99s PacWest for me.=E2=80=9D But the Wall=
 Street Journal reports today that, if the debt ceiling=C2=A0<em>is=C2=A0</=
em>lifted, it=C2=A0<a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click=

he banks again</a>:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Even once the debt ceiling is resolved, there =
may be more plot twists for American companies=E2=80=94especially deposit-h=
ungry banks.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Ending the standoff will enable the U.S. Treas=
ury to raise cash to pay its bills. Normally that is a dull part of the fin=
ancial system=E2=80=99s plumbing, but these aren=E2=80=99t normal times for=
 many banks. And since the Treasury also must refill a huge hole that the m=
ultimonth drama has forced it to dig, the refunding process could add to th=
e competition for the cash of savers and investors. That would exacerbate w=
orries about banks=E2=80=99 funding.</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Once the Treasury can issue more debt again, i=
t=E2=80=99s going to compete even harder for savers=E2=80=99 money.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Also at the Journal, here is =E2=80=9C<a href=
=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/31597233.273415/aHR0cHM6L=

to Prevent Debt-Ceiling Chaos</a>=E2=80=9D:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Wall Street is breaking out its doomsday playb=
ook for how to survive a U.S. default.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The industry=E2=80=99s primary goal: keep the =
financial markets functioning. Many fear everything from computer glitches =
to cascading panic if the U.S. misses payments on Treasurys, which are a be=
drock of trading and usually considered almost as safe as cash.=C2=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Under Wall Street=E2=80=99s plan, though, inve=
stors would be able to keep trading all U.S. Treasurys, even those with pas=

bay through a series of conference calls, each with an agenda already organ=
ized by the Securities Industry and Financial Markets Association trade gro=
up.=C2=A0</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I would say the article is fairly optimistic a=
bout global financial markets continuing to function despite a default, and=
 I too <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/31597233.27=
3415/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjMtMDUtMj=

=E2=80=9D is not <em>completely=C2=A0</em>encouraging? I get it, if you hav=

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The theory is pretty straightforward:</p>

<span>[2]</span>

</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">So confidence in banks is self-fu=

</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">For most companies, this doesn=E2=

ts stock price and just do its business and eventually the stock will sort =
itself out.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">But for banks, it does=
 matter: If it <em>looks like </em>a bank is in trouble, people will pull t=

gin-bottom: 5px;">If it gets in enough trouble, its stock will go to zero.<=
/li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Retail investors posting on messag=

d then gang up with their friends to move that bank=E2=80=99s stock down, t=
hen they can destroy the bank and make a lot of money.</li>

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Ultimately though this is not a very interesti=

<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">A near-record number of shares in regional ban=

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Rookie traders are flooding social media with =

/p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The result? A market that moves from calm to c=

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Before March, individual investors didn=E2=80=

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Stock gyrations can be punishing for any compa=

ositors to withdraw their money, which does.</p>

aid Cem Karsan, founder of Chicago-based investment firm Kai Volatility Adv=

</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The article features =E2=80=9CDaniel Betancour=

<blockquote>

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">PacWest shares plummeted when markets opened, =
and he spotted his chance.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Put options trading in PacWest shares=E2=80=94=

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">In his Discord chat room, traders celebrated, =

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">PacWest depositors were unnerved. The bank lat=

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Did they withdraw their money because of his p=

 And this did not drive PacWest into failure or anything.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Still! Part of me, as an enjoyer of weird fina=

g, though? Also, once you prove it can be done, why stop with one bank?</p>

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">One way to think about stock buybacks is that,=
 if you are a $10 billion company, and you think that in=C2=A0the future yo=
u will be a $50 billion company, you should buy back stock. Your business h=
as a lot of great opportunities to make money, so your stock is a good inve=
stment. It is cheap today and will be expensive later; if you buy stock the=
n you will make money on the trade.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Another way to think about stock buybacks is t=
hat, if you are a $10 billion company, and you think that in the future you=
 will be a $5 billion company, you should buy back stock. If your business =
is shrinking and there are=C2=A0<em>not=C2=A0</em>a lot of great opportunit=
ies to make money, then your company=C2=A0doesn=E2=80=99t need to be as big=
 as it once was. The business doesn=E2=80=99t need the amount of capital th=
at it once did; you do not need to reinvest in capital equipment and resear=
ch and development if your market is shrinking. There is money today, you d=
on=E2=80=99t have any great uses for it, you might as well return it to sha=
reholders. And in the US in 2023, the normal way to return money to shareho=
lders is by buying back stock.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">If another dollar invested in your business =
=E2=80=94=C2=A0in R&D or capital expenditures or whatever =E2=80=94=C2=
=A0would have a 50%=C2=A0return, then you should invest some dollars in you=
r business, but you might also be tempted to buy back some stock because yo=
u are bullish that the stock will go up. If another dollar invested in your=
 business would have a 0% return, then you shouldn=E2=80=99t really invest =
a lot of dollars in your business, and if you have a bunch of dollars you m=
ight as well spend them on buybacks.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">And so if a lot of companies are buying back a=
 lot of stock, but nobody=C2=A0<em>else=C2=A0</em>is buying a lot of stock,=
 that=C2=A0<em>could=C2=A0</em>mean that corporate managers=C2=A0think that=
 their future looks bright and that they will have great performance over t=
he next few years, while professional investors disagree. Or it=C2=A0<em>co=
uld=C2=A0</em>mean that=C2=A0<em>everybody=C2=A0</em>thinks that the future=
 looks kinda meh and companies will not have great returns over the next fe=
w years; professional investors act on that view by selling stock, while co=
rporate managers act on that view by returning capital to shareholders beca=
use they don=E2=80=99t have any better use for it.=C2=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusi=
ness.com/click/31597233.273415/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cud3NqLmNvbS9hcnRpY2xlcy9zaGFy=
ZS1idXliYWNrcy1jb250aW51ZS1hdC10b3JyaWQtcGFjZS13aGlsZS1pbnZlc3RvcnMtc2l0LW9=

9a23cBe6b1a9b3" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_b=
lank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bot=
tom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; b=
ackground-color: #ccc;"> Wall Street Journal reports</a>:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">U.S. companies have remained stalwart buyers o=
f their own shares even as institutional and individual investors alike hav=
e become skittish and trimmed their exposure to equities.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Companies in the Russell 3000 have unveiled pl=
ans to buy back more than $600 billion in shares this year, in line with la=
st year=E2=80=99s record pace, according to data from research firm Birinyi=
 Associates. In all, they announced $1.27 trillion of share repurchases and=
 completed $1.05 trillion in buybacks in 2022, both all-time highs, accordi=
ng to Birinyi.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">That activity has offered an important source =
of support for the stock market. Data on fund flows show many of the tradit=
ional buyers of stocks have been net sellers of late. Some have moved into =
less risky investments like money-market funds amid concerns that the econo=
my is on the brink of a recession.</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Obviously many of the specific stories here ar=
e along the lines of =E2=80=9Cour business is great, we are rolling in mone=
y, we just cannot possibly spend it all and we=E2=80=99re giving some back =
to shareholders.=E2=80=9D But I am not=C2=A0<em>sure=C2=A0</em>that that is=
 the macro story.=C2=A0If you think that the economy is on the=C2=A0brink o=
f a recession and business will be bad, and you are an investor, you might =
want to sell stock. If you think that the economy is on the brink of a rece=
ssion and business will be bad, and you are a company, you might want to bu=
y your stock.</p>

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">He worked with [Nasdaq Inc. Chief Executive Of=
ficer Adena] Friedman and Nasdaq staff on research to show that dual-listed=
 stocks tend to underperform their peers over time. The report analyzed a g=
roup of more than 550 companies with valuations between $10 billion and $50=
 billion, drawing comparisons between stocks listed solely in the US, US AD=
Rs with a foreign listing and US ADRs with a London listing. It concluded t=
hat stocks listed only in the US had 2.5 times greater trading volumes, abo=
ut three times more liquidity and two times higher median price-to-equity v=
aluation.=C2=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=E2=80=9CUS-listed peers have tighter spreads,=
 higher valuation and greater liquidity than US American Depository Receipt=
s with a foreign listing,=E2=80=9D read the report produced for SoftBank, w=
hich was reviewed by Bloomberg. Plus, =E2=80=9Cthere are additional costs t=
o consider,=E2=80=9D such as producing financial statements under US and UK=
 standards, stated the document.=C2=A0</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I just love the idea of the US ambassador to J=
apan writing and presenting bullet points like =E2=80=9CUS-listed peers hav=
e tighter spreads, higher valuation and greater liquidity than US American =
Depository Receipts with a foreign listing.=E2=80=9D</p>
